Metadata.ServiceAgencyName: Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense

Cells.Value: Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense

Text.Value: In accordance with the Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 Appropriation Act Language "for transfer to appropriations available to the Department of War for military personnel of the reserve components serving under the provisions of title 10 and title 32, United States Code; operation and maintenance; for procurement; and for research, development, test, and evaluation" below is the Department of War projected transfer estimates by Public Law and Fiscal Year.

The FY 2027 request for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $918,002 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $918,002 thousand to directly support our program.

 

The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $1,148,675 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $1,148,675 thousand to directly support our program.

 

The FY 2025 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $1,110,436 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $1,110,436 thousand to directly support our program.

 

The FY 2025 Enacted column reflects amounts transferred from the Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense appropriation, a Central Transfer Account, to the Military Departments and Defense Agencies.

Introduction and Overview

 

A.  Requested Fiscal Year 2027 Appropriation Act Language

 

For drug interdiction and counter-drug activities of the Department of War, for transfer to appropriations available to the Department of War for military personnel of the reserve components serving under the provisions of title 10 and title 32, United States Code; operation and maintenance; for procurement; and for research, development, test, and evaluation, $918,002,000:  Provided, That the funds appropriated under this heading shall be available for obligation for the same time period and for the same purpose as the appropriation to which transferred:  Provided further, That upon a determination that all or part of the funds transferred from this appropriation are not necessary for the purposes provided herein, such amounts may be transferred back to this appropriation:  Provided further, That the transfer authority provided under this heading is in addition to any other transfer authority contained elsewhere in this Act.  Provided further, That funds appropriated under this heading may be used to support a new start program or project only after written prior notification to the Committees on Appropriations of the House of Representatives and the Senate. 

 

 

B.  Description of Operations Financed

Funds the Department of War’s Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense appropriation’s four budget activities:  Budget Activity 01) Counter-Narcotics Support, Budget Activity 02) Drug Demand Reduction Program, Budget Activity 03) National Guard Counter-Drug Program, and Budget Activity 04) National Guard Counter-Drug Schools. 

 

Counter-Narcotics Support budget activities includes activities such as detection and monitoring, intelligence analysis, and small-scale construction aimed at the national priority to identify, disrupt, and degrade those transnational criminal networks that pose the greatest threats to U.S. national security.  

 

Drug Demand Reduction Program budget activities include prevention, education, and outreach to ensure the dangers of drug abuse and misuse within the Department of War are understood, prevented, identified, and treated to keep the fighting force at the highest levels of readiness. 

 

National Guard Counter-Drug Program budget activities support State National Guard assistance to Federal, State, tribal, and local law enforcement agencies, and community-based organizations in efforts to disrupt and degrade illicit drug production and trafficking within the United States. 

 

National Guard Counter-Drug Schools budget activities fund the operation of five National Guard Counter-Drug schools which provide Counter-Drug and related counter transnational organized crime courses of instruction to Federal, State, local, and community-based organizations from the 50 states, four territories, and the District of Columbia.   

 

The funds are appropriated to the Department’s Central Transfer Account titled "Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense".  The Central Transfer Account provides for centralized budgeting and oversight while allowing funds to be executed across the entire range of Department of War Military Departments, Combatant Commands, and Combat Support Agencies.  The account plays a critical role in strengthening the Department’s commitment to securing the nation’s borders and aligning with Federal partners to defend our territorial integrity.  Through strategic support and joint operations, it helps repel and disrupt the flow of illicit drugs threatening the Homeland and undermining regional stability.   

 

Under the authority contained in Section 1022 of the FY2004 National Defense Authorization Act, as amended, Department of War components funded by the Central Transfer Account may also, under certain conditions, support other departments and agencies working to counter terrorism and transnational organized crime without a nexus to drug trafficking.  Although activities conducted under Section 1022 represent a small part of the Department’s overall Counter-Drug effort, this authority is used to enable significant effects against the operations and finances of terrorists and transnational organized crime groups that threaten the national security of the United States. 

 

 

C.  Global Threats and Trends

 

The National Defense Strategy directs the Department of War to defend the Homeland, including by sealing our borders and repelling forms of invasion including narcotics trafficking.  The Department of War Counter-Drug activities implement Executive Orders 14157 (“Designating Cartels and Other Organizations as Foreign Terrorist Organizations and Specially Designated Global Terrorists”) and 14167 (“Clarifying the Military’s Role in Protecting the Territorial Integrity of the United States”).   Drug trafficking, transnational organized crime (to include criminal groups designated as Foreign Terrorist Organizations), and other illicit threat networks continue to pose a serious and persistent threat to the security, health, and safety of the American people.  Illicit threat networks include terrorists, criminals, proliferators, and adversary states that present unprecedented asymmetrical threats to U.S. interests at home and abroad.  Only through a unified effort with interagency and foreign partners can we address these complex challenges.  

 

According to provisional data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention National Center for Health Statistics as of March 2026, predicted drug overdose deaths in the United States have continued their significant downward trend.  For the 12-month period ending in October 2025, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention latest Vital Statistics Rapid Release (published March 11, 2026) predict approximately 71,542 overdose deaths, a decline of 17.1% compared to the previous year.   This follows an even sharper drop in 2024, which saw a record 24% annual decline (totaling roughly 79,384 deaths), marking a definitive shift after two decades of rising fatalities. While synthetic opioids—primarily fentanyl—and psychostimulants like methamphetamines remain the leading drivers of these trends, deaths involving synthetic opioids saw their first major sustained decrease during this period. Health officials attribute the "unprecedented" progress to expanded naloxone distribution, better access to medication-assisted treatment, and evolving shifts in the illicit drug supply.

The Sinaloa Cartel and Jalisco New Generation Cartel, now designated as Foreign Terrorist Organizations, are the primary groups producing illicit synthetic drugs and trafficking these drugs into the United States.  These and other cartels produce illicit drugs in clandestine laboratories in Mexico using precursor chemicals and pill presses sourced from companies in China and India.  Much of the fentanyl is pressed into pills resembling legitimate medication and sold to customers in the United States through social media platforms and messaging applications.  These cartels rely on business relationships with drug trafficking organizations in the United States that handle the distribution and sale of drugs, and with Chinese money laundering networks to move drug money quickly and consistently from the United States to Mexico.  The cartels not only fuel the drug poisoning deaths in the United States but also commit egregious acts of violence, threatening the security and stability of our partners across the Western Hemisphere.   

 

China’s semi-permissive legal environment allows it to remain a global hub of illicit activities.  Specifically, China’s role in permitting or enabling drug trafficking and illicit activities, including economic crimes, money laundering, illicit drug trafficking, wildlife trafficking, trafficking in natural resources, and the illicit procurement of sensitive technologies, has only grown in recent years.  China has also proven adept at exploiting the instability and insecurity created by transnational organized crime as it seeks to increase its influence around the world.  India’s role as a source for precursor chemicals has grown in recent years as criminal networks exploit India’s underregulated chemical industry.  Iran and its affiliated networks blend state, economic, criminal, and cyber means to achieve its national objectives.  Russian and other Eurasian transnational criminal organizations continue to thrive within and outside their borders, including in the United States.  The Russian government has shown a capacity and willingness to use criminal means to achieve Russia’s global objectives.  

 

Terrorist organizations and violent extremist organizations remain a threat to U.S. national security.  Terrorists such as Hezbollah, Islamic State of Iraq and Syria, al-Shabaab, and al-Qaeda-affiliated groups such as Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin in the Sahel often engage in criminal activities to fund their attacks, leverage criminal facilitators to move weapons and people, and navigate the global financial system using the same methodologies as transnational criminal organizations such as money laundering and informal value transfers.   

 

Given the significant responsibilities of our military service members and civilian personnel, deterring and detecting drug abuse, and ensuring a drug-free workplace are essential to maintaining Department of War readiness.  As illegal and prescription drugs have become increasingly available within our communities and more states legalize marijuana, a top Department priority is to resource responsive and effective deterrence programs to prevent illegal drug use and prescription drug misuse by the workforce.  

 

 

D.  Strategic Objectives

The Department's statutory mission to detect and monitor aerial and maritime transit of illicit drugs toward the United States remains a priority.  By performing this mission, the Department of War helps secure the homeland by deterring, disrupting, and degrading potential threats before they reach the United States.  This includes supporting interagency and international efforts to target transnational criminal organizations at their source and building international partnerships to prevent transnational threat networks from undermining regional stability and threatening U.S. interests.  The priorities identified below address a range of functional and regional threats, including longstanding challenges posed by illicit drug trafficking, other forms of transnational organized crime, and transnational terrorism financed by illicit means.  For some activities, depending on the nature of the activity and the authority used, the Department of War may have a lead or supporting role. 

The goal of the Department of War Counter-Drug program is to disrupt and degrade the production and trafficking of illicit drugs and precursors, with a primary focus on defending the U.S. southern border and maritime approaches.  Counter-Drug funded components should use the following priorities as a guide when developing and executing operations, activities, and investments.

  • Enhance the effectiveness, operational flexibility, and agility of the detection and monitoring mission, with a focus on a) acquiring innovative, scalable platforms and cross-cutting capabilities leading to immediate interdiction; b) leveraging the Department’s network of enduring and contingency locations in the Western Hemisphere to support multiple homeland defense missions; and c) integrating with other United States Government Counter-Drug assets/architecture/operations to close seams among Combatant Command in the maritime approaches to the United States.

     

  • Disrupt[1] and degrade[2] the operational capacity of cartels, foreign terrorist organizations, and transnational criminal organizations, with a focus on supporting Homeland Security Task Forces. 

     

  • Target illicit synthetic drug supply chains from China, India, or other countries that are responsible for the flow of precursor chemicals, new psychoactive substances, pill presses, counterfeit dies, and other equipment used in the manufacturing of dangerous opioids, methamphetamines, and counterfeit pills into the Western Hemisphere.

  • Target foreign terrorist organizations and transnational criminal organizations financial networks, with a focus on networks exploiting the global financial system utilizing trade and service-based money laundering, false invoicing, and the use of crypto currency, Hawalas, and the Chinese Underground Banking System.

  • Support increased burden sharing by focusing support on partner nation law enforcement and security forces that collaborate with U.S. law enforcement (e.g. Sensitive Investigative Units and Transnational Criminal Investigative Units), with an emphasis on security forces actively contributing to counter-cartel and foreign terrorist organization efforts.

     

  • Support efforts to disrupt and degrade the global networks of Western Hemisphere-based drug cartels and foreign terrorist organizations in Europe, Africa, and Asia, as well as priority transnational criminal organizations based in those regions.

     

  • Support efforts—to include enabling maritime interdiction—that disrupt and degrade terrorist funding and supply chains, with a focus on al-Shabaab, Al-Qaeda and its affiliates, Islamic State of Iraq and Syria and its affiliates, the Haqqani network, and Iran-affiliated organizations.

 

These priorities support the Administration’s Drug Policy Priorities, the National Defense Strategy, and Department of War key responsibilities:  protect the homeland and guarantee the security of the nation’s citizens and territory; enable United States interagency counterparts; and prioritize assistance to partners who are aligned with United States interests.   


[1] Temporarily interrupt or reduce the effectiveness or efficiency of an illicit network or activity e.g., the bulk transportation of illicit drugs or other contraband, chemical precursors, the financial proceeds of illicit trade, the detention, arrest, or seizure of suspects and evidentiary items. 

[2] Temporarily reduce the effectiveness or efficiency of drug trafficking and transnational criminal organizations systems, efforts, or means.  Drug trafficking and transnational criminal organizations are unable to function as a global organization and regress into groups whose expansion is held in check by local and regional security forces, law enforcement, or other element of national power.

 

 

E.  Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense Appropriation and Program Management Background 

 

Since Fiscal Year 1989, the Department of War Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense appropriation has been the principal means to finance the Department’s Counter-Drug and Drug Demand Reduction activities.  The funds are appropriated to the Department’s Counter-Drug Central Transfer Account, which provides for centralized budgeting and oversight while allowing funds to be executed across the entire range of the Department of War Counter-Drug funded components.   

 

In accordance with 31 U.S.C. §1105(a)(26) and 21 U.S.C. §1703(c), this budget justification describes and provides a consolidated view of Department of War contributions to the National Drug Control Strategy and various Executive Orders executed under this appropriation. 

 

The Deputy Assistant Secretary of War for Counternarcotics and Stabilization Policy, with the oversight of the Assistant Secretary of War for Special Operations and Low-Intensity Conflict, and with direction from the Under Secretary of War for Policy, provides policy guidance and oversight, resource allocation, and program assessment functions for the Department’s Counter-Drug, Counter Transnational Organized Crime[3], and Counter Threat Finance[4] activities.

 

This portfolio includes resources budgeted under Budget Activity 01, Counter-Narcotics Support; Budget Activity 03, National Guard Counter-Drug Program; and Budget Activity 04, National Guard Counter-Drug Schools Program.  

 

For the Drug Demand Reduction Program, the Office of Drug Demand Reduction, by direction of the Executive Director for Force Resiliency, and with oversight by the Under Secretary of War for Personnel and Readiness, provides policy oversight and guidance, resource allocation, and reviews and monitors the Combatant Commands, Military Departments, and Combat Support Agencies program execution and effectiveness.  Drug Demand Reduction programs are mandated by Executive Order 12564, 10 U.S.C. §978, other guidance contained in Department of War Directives and Instructions, and the Federal Register.  This portfolio includes resources budgeted under Budget Activity 02, Drug Demand Reduction Program.  

 

Resources requested within Budget Activities 01, 03, and 04 of the Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities appropriation are aligned to priorities detailed in Executive Order 14157 “Designating Cartels And Other Organizations As Foreign Terrorist Organizations And Specially Designated Global Terrorists,” Executive Order 14159 “Protecting the American People Against Invasion,” Executive Order, 14167, "Clarifying the Military's Role in Protecting the Territorial Integrity of the United States," the National Defense Strategy, and other national-level strategy and guidance documents. 

These resources also support requirements presented by the Combatant Commands, Military Departments, and Combat Support Agencies.  The Deputy Assistant Secretary of War for Counternarcotics and Stabilization Policy validates requirements on a recurrent basis via an annual planning, programming, and budgeting process.  Each program is reviewed and evaluated to ensure alignment with the evolving threat environment, Administration priorities, the Department’s strategic objectives, Combatant Commands mission requirements, adherence to legislative authorities, and policy guidance. 


 

______________________________

 

[1]  Temporarily interrupt or reduce the effectiveness or efficiency of an illicit network or activity e.g., the bulk transportation of illicit drugs or other contraband, chemical precursors, the financial proceeds of illicit trade, the detention, arrest, or seizure of suspects and evidentiary items. 

[2] Temporarily reduce the effectiveness or efficiency of drug trafficking and transnational criminal organizations systems, efforts, or means.  Drug trafficking and transnational criminal organizations are unable to function as a global organization and regress into groups whose expansion is held in check by local and regional security forces, law enforcement, or other element of national power.

[3] Counter-Transnational Organized Crime:  Activities to detect, monitor, disrupt, interdict, or degrade transnational criminal organizations and networks trafficking in, or deriving support or resources from, illicit drugs, weapons, natural resources, wildlife, or people that threaten United States interests or cause instability to regional partners. 

[4] Counter Threat Finance:  Activities conducted to deny, disrupt, destroy, or defeat the generation, storage, movement, and use of assets to fund activities that support an adversary’s ability to negatively affect United States interests.

Text.Value: In accordance with the Fiscal Year 2027 Appropriation Act Language "for transfer to appropriations available to the Department of War for military personnel of the reserve components serving under the provisions of title 10 and title 32, United States Code; operation and maintenance; for procurement; and for research, development, test, and evaluation" below is the Department of War projected transfer estimates by Fiscal Year and Public Law.

The FY 2027 request for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $658,191 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $658,191 thousand to directly support the Counter-Narcotics Support program.

 

The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $678,737 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $678,737 thousand to directly support the Counter-Narcotics Support program.

 

The FY 2025 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $653,702 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $653,702 thousand to directly support the Counter-Narcotics Support program.

 

The FY 2025 Enacted column reflects amounts transferred from the Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense appropriation, a Central Transfer Account, to the Military Departments and Defense Agencies.

Counterdrug:  countering the threat posed by illicit drugs and other types of trafficking, focusing on reducing illicit drug availability in the United States; neutralizing threat networks that profit from illicit trafficking and strengthening interagency and international partners’ ability to combat trafficking.

 

Countering Transnational Organized Crime:  countering transnational criminal organizations (including those recently designated as foreign terrorist organizations) and illicit networks with links to terrorist groups or other groups that significantly threaten U.S. national security interests.

 

Counter Threat Finance:  analyzing how adversaries generate, store, move and use money and other resources, and how to disrupt their financial networks using whole-of-government authorities and capabilities.

 

Primary Authorities: 10 U.S.C. §124, §284, §342, §1021 National Defense Authorization Act for 2005, as amended, and §1022 National Defense Authorization Act for 2004, as amended.

Text.Value:
The Office of the Deputy Assistant Secretary of War for Counternarcotics and Stabilization Policy, by direction of the Under Secretary of War for Policy, and with oversight by the Assistant Secretary of War for Special Operations/Low-Intensity Conflict, provides policy oversight, guidance, resources, and the measurement of effects for the Department of War Counter-Drug, Counter-Transnational Organized Crime, and Counter Threat Finance programs and activities.  Individual programs and activities are designed, planned, and executed by the Combatant Commands in cooperation with the listed Combat Support Agencies.  The Military Services provide program execution support and fiscal oversight on behalf of Service component activities executed on behalf of the Combatant Commands.  

 

Office of the Secretary of War:

Under Secretary of War for Policy

Assistant Secretary of War for Special Operations/Low-Intensity Conflict

Deputy Assistant Secretary of War for Counternarcotics and Stabilization Policy

 

Military Services:

Department of the Army

Department of the Navy

United States Marine Corps

National Guard

NarrativeData.UserText: Decrease of $25,853 thousand for a one-time Fiscal Year 2026 Congressional adjustment to the Counter-Drug program.  This funding was designated to enhance drug interdiction efforts by providing commercial Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance support to U.S. Southern Command, and communications satellite imagery artificial intelligence/machine learning.

Cells.Value: 1) U.S. Southern Command Maritime Patrol Aircraft Support

NarrativeData.UserText: Decrease of $2,159 thousand for the divestment of U.S. Southern Command’s Maritime Patrol Aircraft Support Program.  This reflects a strategic shift toward more cost-effective, technologically advanced surveillance capabilities. Partner nations have significantly improved their maritime domain awareness, reducing the need for direct U.S. aircraft support.

Text.Value:
The Department of War supports the nation’s Counterdrug and Counter Transnational Organized Crime efforts as the lead for the detection and monitoring of aerial and maritime drug threats bound for the United States, supporting U.S. law enforcement partners through planning, intelligence analysis, linguistic support, threat finance intelligence, and other activities per 10 U.S.C 284(b).  Support to foreign law enforcement may include small-scale construction, linguistic support and transportation support.  

 

Measures of performance assigned to these activities are used by decision makers to observe progress and measure actual results in comparison to expected results and operational objectives; guide the allocation of counterdrug and counter transnational organized crime budgetary resources during the annual planning, programming, budgeting, and execution process; provide management and oversight of Department of War counterdrug and countering transnational organized crime programs; and facilitate communications and engagements with internal and external stakeholders.

 

The National Drug Control Budget, published by the Office of National Drug Control Policy, is assembled by agency Budget Decision Unit.  The Fiscal Year 2027 Department of War Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense budget request supports one Budget Decision Unit, titled Drug Interdiction and Counterdrug Activities.  Information regarding measures of performance that are compiled and monitored by the Deputy Assistant Secretary of War for Counternarcotics and Stabilization Policy, but not funded by this appropriation (Military Services Operations Tempo), may be found in the Office of National Drug Control Policy National Drug Control Budget.  Selected Fiscal Year 2025 qualitative and quantitative program performance results are provided in the following table, and accompanying narrative, to communicate Department of War's continued progress toward achieving operational objectives in support of the National Drug Control Program.  These measures, including data sources, goals, and results are subject to a Department of War Inspector General Attestation Review at least once every three years to ensure quality standards are reliability. 

 

Selected Measures of FY 2025 Performance                                                                                                                         FY 2025       FY 2025

                                                                                                                                                                                                      Target      Achieved

Budget Decision Unit 1 – Drug Interdiction and Counterdrug Activities

 

Goal: The supply of illicit substances into the United States is reduced.

Measure 1: Narcotics and Transnational Crime Support Center support to Interagency Priority Cases                                     49 cases     57 cases

Measure 2: Percentage of Detected Events Successfully Handed-off to Interdiction and Apprehension Resources                          80%           79%

 

Measure 1: Narcotics and Transnational Crime Support Center support to Interagency Priority Cases.

The Department of War counterdrug Enterprise-Wide Intelligence programs broadly support and enhance the capabilities of the Department of War components in performing counterdrug, counter transnational organized crime, and counter threat finance missions to help U.S. and international law enforcement partners deter, disrupt, and defeat global drug-trafficking, illicit finance, transnational crime, and terrorist activities. The Narcotics and Transnational Crime Support Center facilitates tailored analytical support to law enforcement, interagency and Department of War efforts to address transnational organized crime issues by providing intelligence analysis support, as requested, to active cases and operations.

 

In Fiscal Year (FY) 2025, the Narcotics and Transnational Crime Support Center supported 57 high-priority operations, exceeding its target of 49 by 14%.  Notably, 128 of 152, or 84%, of Narcotics and Transnational Crime Support Centers total supported operations were focused against Administration priorities and Executive Orders including Mexican Cartels designated as Foreign Terrorist Organization and Specially Designated Global Terrorist to include illicit drug supply chains and illicit financing.  Through increased outreach with select law enforcement agencies, the Narcotics and Transnational Crime Support Center increased its support to new Homeland Security Task Force designated cases from 0 in FY 2024 to 12 in FY 2025.  These Homeland Security Task Force cases supplant former Homeland Security Investigations HOMECORT and Homeland Security Investigations Strategic Network Dismantlement Project cases, as well as subsume many Organized Crime Drug Enforcement Task Forces designations.

Measure 2:  Percent of Detected Events Successfully Handed-off to Interdiction and Apprehension Resources

Percentage of Western Hemisphere illicit trafficking events, as estimated by interagency and international intelligence activities, detected and successfully handed-off to interdiction and apprehension arrests by Joint Interagency Task Force-South. A successful hand-off is defined as positive communications with Interdiction and Apprehension resources providing cueing about a drug trafficking target.

 

In FY 2025, Joint Interagency Task Force-South’s detection and monitoring efforts led to the seizure of over 450 metric tons of cocaine while also remanding 219 traffickers to Department of Justice custody.  Performance measure results indicate a high level of efficiency and effectiveness in command and control, with nearly 80% of detected events successfully handed off to interdiction resources.  While slightly shy of its 80% (79%) performance target for FY 2025, Joint Interagency Task Force-South demonstrated a considerable improvement over the 70% achieved in FY 2024.  To improve performance, Joint Interagency Task Force-South focused on enhancing internal processes for more rapid information sharing with partners and leveraging innovative technologies to optimize detection capabilities. The strategic deployment of U.S. Government assets such as maritime patrol aircraft to the Galapagos Islands, as well as a late Fiscal Year surge in U.S. Coast Guard and U.S. Navy assets, also helped contribute to positive performance this year.

Metadata.ServiceAgencyName: Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense

Text.Value: In accordance with the Fiscal Year 2027 Appropriation Act Language "for transfer to appropriations available to the Department of War for military personnel of the reserve components serving under the provisions of title 10 and title 32, United States Code; operation and maintenance; for procurement; and for research, development, test, and evaluation" below is the Department of War projected transfer estimates by Fiscal Year and Public Law.

The FY 2027 request for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $135,745 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $135,745 thousand to directly support the Drug Demand Reduction program.

 

The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $134,938 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $134,938 thousand to directly support the Drug Demand Reduction program.

 

The FY 2025 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $135,567 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $135,567 thousand to directly support the Drug Demand Reduction program.

 

The FY 2025 Enacted column reflects amounts transferred from the Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense appropriation, a Central Transfer Account, to the Military Departments and Defense Agencies.

The Drug Demand Reduction budget activity funds programs to ensure the dangers of drug abuse and misuse within the Department of War are understood, prevented, identified, and treated to keep the fighting force at the highest levels of readiness.  Prevention, education, and outreach programs focus on Department of War’s military and civilian communities designed to raise awareness of the adverse consequences of illicit drug use and prescription drug misuse on job performance, safety, health, family stability, fiscal security, and employment opportunities.  Programs include illicit drug awareness and life-skills training for Service members and their families with a focus on maintaining readiness by ensuring a drug free workforce and workplace.  Outreach services include programs to assist military and civilian employees in locating Department of War and local civilian agency resources to address substance abuse, dependency, or addiction.  In addition to prevention and outreach efforts, this budget activity funds personnel, supplies, and materials that support installation level random drug testing specimen collection for all military service members, civilian personnel in testing designated positions, and pre-employment testing for applicants for military and civilian service.  The Department of War operates five forensic drug testing laboratories (U.S. Army, Fort Meade, MD; U.S. Army, Tripler AMC, HI; U.S. Navy, Great Lakes IL; U.S. Navy, Jacksonville, FL; and U.S. Air Force, Lackland, TX).  These laboratories conduct urinalysis testing for the presence of illegal drugs, novel synthetic drugs, and unauthorized prescription medications listed in the Department of War drug test panel.  Funding supports laboratory personnel costs and overhead expenses related to test reagents, disposable supplies, and support services.  The Fort Meade forensic testing laboratory is certified by the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration to analyze specimens from Department of War Agency civilians and civilian applicants in testing designated positions under the Drug-Free Workplace Program.  The Great Lakes, IL forensic testing laboratory processes urinalysis samples for all military applicants from 65 Military Entrance Processing Stations worldwide.  The Lackland, TX forensic testing laboratory processes the majority of National Guard Bureau Service member specimens.

 

Primary Authorities: 10 U.S.C. §978, 5 U.S.C. §7301.

Text.Value:
The Department of War Drug Demand Reduction Program enables operational readiness and safety of the Total Force by deterring drug abuse through random drug testing; surveillance of emerging drug threats; prevention, education, and outreach efforts. 

 

Measures of performance assigned to these activities are used by decision makers to:  observe progress and measure actual results in comparison to expected results and operational objectives; guide the allocation of counterdrug and counter transnational organized crime budgetary resources during the annual planning, programming, budgeting, and execution process; provide management and oversight of Department of War counterdrug and countering transnational organized crime programs; and facilitate communications and engagements with internal and external stakeholders.

 

The National Drug Control Budget, published by the Office of National Drug Control Policy, is assembled by agency Budget Decision Units.  The FY 2027 Department of War Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense budget request supports one Budget Decision Unit, titled Drug Interdiction and Counterdrug Activities.  Information regarding measures of performance that are compiled and monitored by the Under Secretary of War for Personnel and Readiness, may be found in the Office of National Drug Control Policy National Drug Control Budget.  Selected examples of FY 2025 qualitative and quantitative program performance results are provided in the following table, and accompanying narrative, to communicate Department of War's continued progress toward achieving operational objectives in support of the National Drug Control Program.  These measures, including data sources, goals, and results are subject to a Department of War Inspector General Attestation Review at least once every three-years to ensure quality standards and reliability.

 

Measure 1:  Department of War military personnel testing positive for drug use.

The Department of War's Drug Demand Reduction Program was mandated in 1981 and given the mission to deter Department of War personnel from abusing illicit drugs or misusing prescription drugs.  The program components include compulsory random drug testing with punitive consequences and anti-drug education and outreach programs.  The effectiveness of this program is measured by monitoring the prevalence of drug use from drug testing statistics published annually with a 2% or less urine drug positive rate for military personnel and to ensure 100% urine drug testing of every Service member is conducted annually.   These goals were established as Well-Being of the Force Indicators in 2008 [1].  An additional source of determining the effectiveness of the Drug Demand Reduction Program is the Department of War Survey of Health-Related Behaviors.  The Department of War survey is conducted every three years as an additional measure of effectiveness because it is independent from the drug testing program.  The specific metric from the survey monitored is self-reported use of illicit drugs and misuse of prescription drugs within the past 30 days. 

 

In Fiscal Year 2025 the percent of military personnel testing positive for drug use was 1.43%, 0.57% below the goal of 2% with a random testing rate of 176%.

 

Measure 2:  Department of War civilian personnel testing positive for drug use. 

The Department of War is on track to keep the illicit drug positive rate below 1% for civilian personnel, despite the Department expanding the drug testing panel to include commonly abused prescription drugs and synthetic marijuana (also known as Spice). Given the success of Department of War's civilian drug testing program, the random testing rate for civilians in testing-designated positions will be 50% of the workforce per year. 

 

In Fiscal Year 2025 the percent of civilian personnel testing positive for drug use was 0.55%, 0.45% below the goal of 1% with a random testing rate of 53%. 

 

 

______________________________

[1] Established in response to the Office of the Under Secretary of War (Personnel and Readiness) memorandum, "Well-Being of the Force Indicators", dated 3 March 2008.  Goals were set at values substantially lower than what was reported in the Department of War's Surveys of Health-Related Behavior Among Military Personnel.

Metadata.ServiceAgencyName: Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense

Text.Value: In accordance with the Fiscal Year 2027 Appropriation Act Language "for transfer to appropriations available to the Department of War for military personnel of the reserve components serving under the provisions of title 10 and title 32, United States Code; operation and maintenance; for procurement; and for research, development, test, and evaluation" below is the Department of War projected transfer estimates by Fiscal Year and Public Law.

The FY 2027 request for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $117,418 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $117,418 thousand to directly support the National Guard Counter-Drug program.

 

The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $305,000 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $305,000 thousand to directly support the National Guard Counter-Drug program.

 

The FY 2025 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $295,000 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $295,000 thousand to directly support the National Guard Counter-Drug program.

 

The FY 2025 Enacted column reflects amounts transferred from the Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense appropriation, a Central Transfer Account, to the Military Departments and Defense Agencies.

The National Guard Counter-Drug Program budget activity exclusively funds the Governors State Plans Program (Project 7403) in the form of drug interdiction and counterdrug activities plans submitted by U.S. State Governors in accordance with 32 U.S.C. §112(c) and approved by the Under Secretary of War for Policy per 32 U.S.C. §112(d). 

 

Support multi-jurisdictional task forces, fusion centers and High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas, to include newly established Homeland Security Task Forces in the identification of criminal networks’ key nodes and development of targets to disrupt and dismantle threat networks. Analysts act as force multipliers and bridge gaps between state, local and federal law enforcement partners.

 

Provide ground and aerial reconnaissance using organic military assets to assist law enforcement in identifying drug trafficking and cartel patterns for obtaining and executing arrest warrants. 

 

Funds may be used for the following expenses: pay, allowances, clothing, subsistence, gratuities, travel, and related expenses, as authorized by State law, of personnel of the National Guard of that State used, while not in Federal service, for the purpose of drug interdiction and counterdrug activities; Operation and maintenance of the equipment and facilities of the National Guard of that State used for the purpose of drug interdiction and counterdrug activities; and procurement of services and equipment, and the leasing of equipment, for the National Guard of that State used for the purpose of drug interdiction and counterdrug activities.

 

Primary Authorities:  32 U.S.C. §112, National Guard, Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities.

Text.Value:
32 U.S.C. § 112, National Guard, Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities authorized the Department of War to provide support to United States law enforcement agencies (Federal, State, Local, Tribal, and Coalition-Based Organizations), to detect and disrupt drug trafficking and other illicit threat network activities.  Key Performance Indicator:  The Department of War National Guard Counter-Drug Programs support to the High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area programs is a highlight/point of interest for The Office of National Drug Control Policy and Congress.  The program provides active analytical support to the 33 regional High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas enabling a persistent support mechanism for U.S. law enforcement partners to target, deter and disrupt illicit actors. Beginning in 2023 the Department of War initiated two new performance metrics to track National Guard support to the High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas. The first metric measures the overall number of High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area missions supported and the second tracks the number of National Guard supported High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area-centric drug seizures conducted.

 

Measures of Performance                                                                                            FY 2024      FY 2025      FY 2026

National Guard Support to High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area Missions                         1,088          1,701           1,701

National Guard Support to High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area-centric Drug Seizures     1,013          1,574           1,574

From FY 2024 to FY 2025, the number of National Guard State-Level support to National Guard Support to High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area missions increased by 56%.  This growth is attributed to several factors, including improved data entry processes, increased program funding realized late in FY 2024 and sustained into FY 2025, and the inclusion of the Drug Demand Reduction mission.  As a supporting entity, the National Guard’s involvement in National Guard Support to High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area missions remains contingent on National Guard Support to High Intensity Drug Trafficking Area requirements.  The National Guard Bureau will continue to prioritize support for National Guard Support to High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas through enhanced national-level engagement, regional coordination, and updated State Plans guidance. Additionally, National Guard Support Leading to Drug Seizures increased by 55% from FY 2024 to FY 2025.  This rise in seizure activity is primarily due to improved data entry processes and increased mission support. The reporting does not specify seizure quantities or the total number of incidents.  Moving forward, the National Guard Bureau will continue to enhance database management practices to best quantify the contributions of National Guard counterdrug support to the National Guard Support to High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas.

Text.Value: In accordance with the Fiscal Year 2027 Appropriation Act Language "for transfer to appropriations available to the Department of War for military personnel of the reserve components serving under the provisions of title 10 and title 32, United States Code; operation and maintenance; for procurement; and for research, development, test, and evaluation" below is the Department of War projected transfer estimates by Fiscal Year and Public Law.

The FY 2027 request for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $6,648 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $6,648 thousand to directly support the National Guard Counter-Drug Schools program.

 

The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $30,000 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $30,000 thousand to directly support the National Guard Counter-Drug Schools program.

 

The FY 2025 spend plan amount for Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense includes $26,167 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory for a total of $26,167 thousand to directly support the National Guard Counter-Drug Schools program.

 

The FY 2025 Enacted column reflects amounts transferred from the Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities, Defense appropriation, a Central Transfer Account, to the Military Departments and Defense Agencies.

The National Guard Counter-Drug Schools budget activity funds the operation of five National Guard Counterdrug Schools as authorized by §901 of the Office of National Drug Control Policy Reauthorization Act of 2006, as amended, and as identified in plans submitted by host State Governors to the Secretary of War in accordance with 32 USC §112(c).  The five schools supported by the program are: 1) Midwest Counterdrug Training Center at Camp Dodge, IA; 2) Multijurisdictional Counterdrug Task Force Training program at Camp Blanding, FL; 3) Northeast Counterdrug Training Center at Fort Indiantown Gap, PA; 4) Regional Counterdrug Training Academy at Naval Air Station Meridian, MS and; 5) Western Regional Counterdrug Training Center at Camp Murray, WA.  The schools leverage existing Department of War infrastructure and capacity to provide no-cost counterdrug training to Federal, State, Local, and Tribal Law Enforcement agencies, as well as community-based organizations and military personnel to build U.S. partner capabilities and capacities to conduct enhanced operations against illicit drug trafficking networks, cartels, and transnational criminal organizations that threaten U.S. national security interest and the public safety of U.S. citizens.  Funds support reserve military pay for Army and Air National Guard personnel, supplies, maintenance, and other administrative costs.

 

Primary Authorities: 32 U.S.C. §112, National Guard, Drug Interdiction and Counter-Drug Activities.

Text.Value:
The Office of the Deputy Assistant Secretary of War for Counternarcotics and Global Threats, by direction of the Under Secretary of War for Policy, and with oversight by the Assistant Secretary of War for Special Operations/Low-Intensity Conflict, provides policy oversight, guidance, resources, and the measurement of effects for Department of War counter-drug programs and activities.  Individual programs and activities are designed, planned, and executed by the 54 National Guards of the U.S. States, Territories, and District of Columbia.  The National Guard Bureau; Headquarters, Department of the Army; Headquarters, Army National Guard; Headquarters, Department of the Air Force; and Headquarters, Air National Guard provide program execution support and fiscal oversight on behalf of activities executed by the States, Territories, and District of Columbia.

Federal Acquisition Regulation, Part 2, Subpart, 2.1, Section 2.101 - Definitions:

Federally Funded Research and Development Centers (FFRDC’s) means activities that are sponsored under a broad charter by a Government agency (or agencies) for the purpose of performing, analyzing, integrating, supporting, and/or managing basic or applied research and/or development, and that receive 70 percent or more of their financial support from the Government; and-

(1)  A long-term relationship is contemplated;

(2)  Most or all of the facilities are owned or funded by the Government; and

(3)  The FFRDC has access to Government and supplier data, employees, and facilities beyond that common in a normal contractual relationship.
